data.title: Alphabet Earnings Preview: Q1 2026
data.description:
CDS swap spreads increase

Source: Christopher Fenske, Primary Markets Group, S&P Global (April 24, 2026)

Alphabet earnings preview: What’s happening to margins?

According to Visible Alpha consensus, Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) (GOOGL) total revenues expected for Q1 2026 have increased to $106.9 billion from $101.5 billion in the fall, driven by resilience in its ad business and potential strength in Google Cloud.

In addition, the Q1 consensus expectations for operating income and EPS have also increased, due to a higher Cloud margin expectation. Since October 2025, the Google Cloud margin has expanded from 20% to 27%, driving consensus EPS expectations up from $2.46 to $2.73 for Q1.

There are differing assumptions around costs, particularly in its Cloud business, leading the Google Cloud margin to range from 11.6% to 34%. It will be interesting to hear what Alphabet says about the outlook and where the Cloud margin is likely to settle.

We are closely monitoring the trend of the Cloud business. The operating profit margin has been trending better. Looking ahead to Q2 2026, analysts now expect the Cloud business to generate a 29.8% operating profit margin, up 900 bps year over year.

Longer term, analysts are also split in their views. For the Cloud business, Visible Alpha consensus expects the operating profit margin to hit 29.4% in FY 2026, but with a significant range of estimates. If the core Search and Ads business remains resilient, the performance of Alphabet stock is likely to be driven by the Cloud segment once again.

We are closely watching what the company will say about its investments into AI, as Alphabet’s FY 2026 CapEx numbers have continued to increase. According to consensus projections, CapEx estimates have surged over 5x from $32.3 billion in FY 2023 to $179.3 billion in FY 2026. While Alphabet’s overall debt remains healthy, it is worth noting that levels are increasing. Also, the market has been critical of the excessive use of cash without a clear ROIC.

Alphabet stock has traded up 35.4% since the October release. The consensus P/E for 2027 is 29x with a target price of $381. The stock has remained resilient, driven by consistent growth in ads and by margin expansion in its Cloud business. Could the Q1 release provide more visibility into the trajectory of 2026 profitability and give shares a further boost?

data.title: Network Effects: The Moat Competitors Can't Match
data.description:
Allen Good - An economic moat is a structural feature that allows a firm to sustain excess returns, and few sources are as powerful as a network effect. At its core, a network effect occurs when the value of a good or service increases for both new and existing users as more customers join the network. This creates a virtual cycle where increased value draws in more users, further strengthening the company's competitive position.

Unlike traditional supply-side economies of scale that focus on production volume, network effects are driven by demand-side economies. The true power of the moat lies in the fact that the value of the network increases exponentially, often as a square of the connections, while the cost to maintain it only increases linearly.

Once this flywheel effect is triggered, customer acquisition costs typically decrease, allowing the company to generate substantial economic profits. We see this manifest in several ways.

Direct networks like WhatsApp become more attractive to newcomers as the existing user base grows. Two-sided marketplaces such as Amazon (AMZN) or Uber (UBER) thrive because more buyers attract more sellers and vice versa.

We also see indirect or direct networks like CrowdStrike (CRWD), where data from one customer is used to improve the security posture for the entire client base. Finally, platforms like the Apple (AAPL) App Store create a massive ecosystem where developers and users are mutually locked in. However, being a popular product isn't enough to claim a moat. To be a sustainable advantage, a network must achieve critical mass, whether its value to the user exceeds the value of the individual product or competitor.

Furthermore, it must be effectively monetized. As we've seen with Twitter or X, a network can grow its user base consistently but fail to be a moat source if it cannot translate that growth into actual profit. While rare, appearing in only about 6% of Morningstar's coverage, network effects represent over 21 trillion in market cap, proving they are gold standard for long-term defensibility.

Brian Colello - Airbnb (ABNB) is a classic example of a network effects business. Network effects occur when the value of a particular good or service increases for both new and existing users as more customers use it. Airbnb has a marketplace network of renters on one side and consumers or visitors on the other.

As more properties come into the network, it attracts more consumers. As more consumers use Airbnb, it makes it more attractive for property owners to join. In both cases, not only do the new property owners or consumers benefit, but the incumbents do too.

The first piece of these types of network effect businesses is getting a jumpstart. This is often discussed as the cold start problem or the chicken and the egg theory. Usually a marketplace network has to focus more on attracting one side of the network than the other. In Airbnb's case, it had to attract a sufficient number of renters before the network offered enough value. From there, in order to have a wide economic moat from network effects, the business has to generate excess returns on capital.

We see two main stumbling blocks for network effect businesses that haven't carved out a wide moat. First again is critical mass. So the business has to be big enough, or there has to be a big enough network to cover the operating costs. Second is effective monetization thereafter. For a marketplace like Airbnb, we think it's a bit straightforward, as it involves collecting a reasonable fee on transactions, although users on both sides have to see enough value in a network to pay those fees. And that's not always the case with lesser networks.

Outside of a marketplace, there are also monetization issues. For a direct network like social media, it often requires effective pricing on ads. For some businesses, like Google Search, they benefit from indirect network effects where the data allows for a better product to attract more users, which improves the data even further, and the flywheel continues.

data.title: Insight Enterprises: Looking For Greater Insights
data.description:
Shares of Insight Enterprises (NSIT) have been suffering recently, with the global technology and IT business suffering both in terms of the business performance and the share price. Shares are down two-thirds from the peak, and while this followed significant momentum beforehand and sales have fallen some 20% from the peak, earnings come in at record levels.

This makes me upbeat, and while 2025 has been a bit messy with some debt added here, I am intrigued by the setback. Nondemanding valuations and the prospect of growth returning in 2026 make me upbeat, yet I am still hard-pressed to have the conviction to get involved in size here.

Other serial special situations and AI beneficiaries are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Another Soft Year

In February, Insight reported a 5% fall in 2025 sales to $8.25 billion, as fourth quarter sales were down just 1% to $2.05 billion, yet the topline pressure really requires some explanations. High-margin service revenues rose by nearly 2% to $1.72 billion, this business posting 9% growth in 2024, with product sales down 7% to $6.53 billion.

Weakness was seen driven by the core US operations, where total sales were down 6% to $6.65 billion, with segment margins reported around 4.2% of sales. The EMEA region reported a 4% fall in full-year sales to $1.36 billion, with segment margins reported in the low 2s. The only bright spot was a 2% increase in APAC sales to $237 million, which makes up nearly 3% of sales here; this, however, is a very profitable segment with operating margins seen near 10% of sales.

Amidst the uneven results, the company posted GAAP earnings of $157 million; this equates to $4.86 per share based on a diluted share count of just over 32 million shares for the year. Adjusted for amortization charges and other items, adjusted earnings came in at $9.87 per share, up nineteen cents on the year before.

Valuation Discussions

Driven by buybacks and the retiring of convertible debt, the diluted share count has fallen to 31 million shares. Trading at $73, the market value stands at $2.26 billion. The balance sheet has taken a hit during the year, amidst ballooning accounts receivable, among others.

Accounts receivables (excluding the long-term receivables) rose by a third to some $5.5 billion, as the company ended the year with $358 million in cash, and with $1.36 billion in debt, for a $1 billion net debt load. This is a substantial amount, with adjusted EBITDA for the year reported at $542 million, for a leverage ratio just shy of 2 times.

Higher debt was the result of convertible bonds being bought back, warrants settled, poor cash flow conversion, share buybacks, and acquisitions. However, the valuations are very reasonable, with the company currently valued at $3.3 billion on an enterprise value basis.

For the year 2026, the company issued an adjusted diluted earnings guidance of $10.10-$10.60 per share, which is inclusive of stock-based compensation expenses. That suggests more growth on this front, but unfortunately no commentary on the sales guidance has been given.

Some growth is likely, as gross margins are seen at similar levels in 2026, with total gross profit growth being guided for. Note that the earnings guidance includes the assumption of $75 million in buybacks, beginning in the first quarter.

Some Perspective

The reality is that Insight has seen solid growth over the past decade, yet lately growth has been underwhelming. A $5.5 billion business a decade ago saw peak revenues at $10.4 billion in 2022, as revenues have fallen to just over $8 billion here, with sequential revenue declines reported ever since. That only tells part of the story, as historically low operating margins around 3% of sales rose to 4% at the peak, to nearly 5% here. The shift to a service business hurts sales but helps margins, with operating profits pretty flattish in dollar terms in recent times.

With about 15% of the share count being bought back over this period of time, investors in the stock have been in for a wild ride. A $30 stock a decade ago peaked in the low $200s in 2024, with shares having fallen some two-thirds ever since, back to levels first seen in 2020.

However, adjusted earnings have been rising further to record levels, and topline sales growth is seen returning in 2026. This is in part driven by inorganic actions taken as well, with Insight announcing the purchase of Inspire11 in October of last year. Inspire is described as a leading Systems Integrator to deliver measurable ROI from AI technologies, a critical industry challenge.

Its solutions are focused on capabilities, data expertise, and an outcome-driven approach, designed to close the AI gap. Little has been said about the other financial implications, yet the cash flow statement for the 2025 results reveals that nearly $300 million was spent on acquisition, likely largely driven by this purchase and that of Sekuro.

What Now?

The reality is that with manageable leverage, growing earnings, and shares lagging, shares trade around 7 times adjusted earnings here. While the earnings are highly adjusted, most of the adjustments relate to amortization charges, which makes me pleased to consider the shares here at a low earnings multiple amidst modest leverage.

Fortunately, cash flow from operations is seen around $300-$400 million this year, north of adjusted earnings. This results in a real value play here, just at a time when the markets are setting fresh highs, including its peers. The reality is that while I have some long-term concerns given the revenue declines in recent times, Insight might actually become a prime acquisition target here.

Based on its current enterprise valuation, shares trade around 0.4 times sales here, as a >$3 billion deal looks very manageable for larger peers like Arrow Electronics (ARW) and TD SYNNEX (SNX). These trade at similar sales multiples, albeit that these larger peers have somewhat lower margins, attributable to Insight's more profitable service segment, depressing its earnings multiple versus its peers.

Given the past soft topline sales performance, I am a bit skeptical about Insight, yet part of this is a deliberate strategy, as growing profits, a return to sales growth, and modest valuations make me rather upbeat here.

However, I fail to have enough conviction at this point in time to initiate a position in size here.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Lower Price Improves Upside, But Uncertainty Remains
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In the last article, I downgraded the rating from Buy to Hold, as the stock at that time reached the fair value that I indicated back in September - $353. I recommended to take a closer look at buying Constellation Energy (CEG) shares only after the price correction is 10% or higher. The correction I expected has occurred; the shares are now worth $313, having decreased in price by exactly 10%. At the same time, my thesis was to buy shares on correction in case the situation in the company persists or even improves. Yes, the fundamental drivers of business growth remain, but since then, market expectations and management forecasts have worsened amid lower revenue growth rates. The price drop followed an increase in uncertainty and the formation of more conservative guidance on the part of management. In my estimation, there remains a slight upside to the current price by 10-12% in the baseline scenario, but for now I prefer to keep the Hold rating, given that the level of risk and uncertainty has increased with the price reduction.

Revenue Growth Driven by Higher Prices, Not Volumes

The latest key event in the company's business is related to the release of the Q4 financial statements and the market's reaction to the demonstrated results. I would like to analyze key aspects of the reporting so that further forecasts for Q1 from the next chapter (the report is due out in May) are more understandable to the reader. But before that, I recommend that you study my first two articles about the company, where I have analyzed in detail all the company's activities, its business model, and its development strategy.

The Q4 results, frankly, leave much to be desired. Let's start with the revenue. There was a 12.9% yoy increase to $6.074 billion. The consensus forecast was 8.42% lower than the actual result, which at first glance is encouraging. But there are nuances. Investors didn't like the fact that almost all of the revenue growth was due to rising electricity prices, but they would like sales to grow. The total volume of electricity supplied and sold decreased to 64.2 TWh from 65.0 TWh in Q4'2024. It turns out that Constellation sold an even smaller volume but received more money for it. Indeed, if you look at the reference prices, you can see that prices have increased dramatically: in PJM West, the price increased by 66% yoy to $57.87 per MWh; in COMEDY, there was an increase of 51% yoy to $37.37; and in New York Central, an increase of 52% to $63.00. Such a high increase in electricity prices is due to a shortage of capacity, high demand from data centers, which are becoming more and more numerous in the United States, as well as partly rising gas prices.

It is also important to understand that part of the overdrive from high prices was offset by a sharp drop in PTC revenue to $320 million from $2.08 billion in 2024. This is due to the fact that tax credits for nuclear generation help at times when electricity prices are falling, and when they are rising strongly, the assistance is reduced. As a result, we have a double-digit revenue growth rate, which was more likely due to a favorable macroeconomic environment rather than due to business growth or development. This has alarmed investors and analysts. Yes, at the moment Constellation remains one of the best in the sector in terms of revenue growth, but what happens if electricity prices fall back?

And it was not possible to convert all these revenues from high prices into high operating profit. Because revenue growth turned out to be slower than the growth in expenses for the purchase of electricity and fuel. While revenue increased by $692 million or 12% yoy, expenses increased by $1.007 billion or 39% yoy. Accordingly, the operating margin fell from 18.1% to 9.8%. Consolidated RNF (revenue minus expenses for purchased electricity and fuel) decreased by 16.4% to $2.48 billion. Nuclear generation has also contributed to the drop in operating margins. Nuclear output barely grew, Capacity factor decreased to 93.1% from 94.8%, and unscheduled downtime increased from 3 days to 30. This further worsened the margin.

GAAP net income decreased even more due to additional revaluations and expenses related to the Calpine transaction. The drop turned out to be more than 50%, to $432 million, and EPS fell to $1.38 from $2.71. In addition to the deterioration in operating results, paper losses were added here, which eventually collapsed the entire net profit. Adjusted operating earnings decreased to only $2.30 from $2.44 per share. The consensus expected $2.25, which is 2.41% lower than the actual result. But these are all accounting profits, and investors are more interested in cash flows, from which dividends will be paid and share buybacks are carried out. The company did not give a specific figure for FCF, but if we subtract the capital expenditure of $986 million from the OCF of $805 million, then roughly calculating it, we get a negative FCF or near zero for Q4. Capital expenditures remain high, which is logical—the business needs to build, participate in projects, and develop and maintain current assets. But if the revenue growth rate does not justify these large capital expenditures, which could go to shareholders in the form of dividends, then the question arises about the effectiveness of the work done.

Growth Expected, but Uncertainty Remains

The Q1 financial statements are coming out soon, and it is important to formulate realistic expectations for quarterly results. Unfortunately, the management does not give its quarterly forecasts. Expectations have been published only based on the results of the entire year. Management aims to earn about $11.00–$12.00 adjusted operating earnings (AOE) per share. At the lower limit, this is an increase of 17% yoy. For comparison, the consensus forecast is $11.61, with an increase of 23.62% yoy - within the framework of the management forecast but closer to the upper limit. But the nuance is that the company divides this forecast into base earnings, that is, the most predictable part, and enhanced earnings, that is, additional profit over base earnings. And enhanced earnings make up almost 40% of the total result, which makes the overall forecast quite volatile, as this is a less reliable part of the profit. In addition, management is going to increase CapEx to $3.9 billion, which will be used for growth projects.

Against this background, it is interesting to study the entire market forecast. The consensus for the whole of 2026 assumes revenue growth of 25.05% yoy to $31.93 billion and, as I have already written, an increase in AOE of 23.62% yoy to $11.61. For the first quarter, the market expects revenue growth of 32.45% yoy to $8.99 billion and AOE of 19.69% yoy to $2.56. Market expectations: In general, they agree with management's comments regarding the full year 2026. In my opinion, the first quarter will be slightly better than expected in terms of revenue growth and operating margin recovery. To achieve this goal, the company needs electricity prices to remain high, business margins to recover, and Calpine integration to start producing results. Given that electricity prices remain high, the task seems feasible. I expect the company to earn about $8.99-9.05 billion and an AOE of about $2.55-2.58 per share in Q1.

Upside Exists, but Execution Matters

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030), and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted, which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have kept my DCF model healthy and conservative. The revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 is about 4.18%, which is a normalization of growth rates after a jump in revenue in 2026-2027. The introduction of additional capacities and the integration of newly acquired assets will support revenue growth. Operating profit will grow from 11% in 2025 to 15% in 2030 due to reduced pressure from fuel prices and synergies with Calpine. At the same time, other operating costs will increase, which will restrain stronger margin growth. Capital expenditures will increase to 12-13% of revenue in order for the company to be able to simultaneously participate in growth projects and maintain current assets. Thus, FCF Yield 2026-2030 will be about 4.73% versus 4.67% for the period 2021-2025.

I got the WACC for Constellation Energy at 8.70% due to a beta of 1.19x, an equity risk premium (taken from the Damodaran table) of 4.3% for USA, and a 4.34% risk-free rate, which I took as 10-year USA Government Bond Yields. The cost of the debt was 5.2%, based on the information in the company's presentation.

Premiums to peers on almost all multiples remain, but they have decreased significantly over the past 2-3 months due to a drop in the share price. The company has been trading at a premium for a long time, and this is normal—investors expect more from Constellation than from peers. And in the last few years, the company has grown faster than most peers and remains one of the strongest companies in terms of business profitability. Premiums to the median for the sector do not mean that stocks cannot grow—companies with such parameters always trade at a premium. We also need to add the factor of the AI boom and the construction of data centers, the beneficiary of which is to some extent Constellation, since the rapid growth of this industry requires the consumption of large amounts of electricity. Right now, all stocks that are beneficiaries of AI growth in one way or another are trading at a premium to the sector.

Since my assumptions in my DCF model have not changed much, the fair value has remained almost the same. In the basic scenario, the stock should be more expensive by 10-13%, and in the positive scenario, by 18-21% in general. In the most negative scenario (with WACC rising and multiples falling), the fair value of the shares should be 4-7% higher than the current market price. That is, there is no downside even in the worst-case scenario. Despite the existing upside, I prefer to keep the Hold rating for now, as the risks of weak financial results have increased, the predictability of future revenue and profit has even worsened, and revenue growth rates leave much to be desired. After the release of the Q1 report, a lot will become clearer, and it will become clearer how to act. Therefore, I do not recommend buying shares before the financial statements are released. As soon as the results are published, I will write another article summarizing my forecast, analyzing the results of the quarter, and giving a new forecast.

Higher Debt, Lower Free Cash Flow for Shareholders

After the closing of the deal with Calpine, the debt burden on the business increased: as of March 31, 2026, the total long-term debt was about $17.5 billion, of which $11.2 billion was corporate debt and $6.3 billion was subsidiary debt. Despite this, the company does not plan to reduce the payment of profits to shareholders. At the end of March, the company increased the total available buyback authorization to $5.0 billion and bought back 17.4 million shares. The dividend was increased by 10% to $0.4265 with a yield of 0.51% and a payout ratio of about 16.5%. Given that the company will now have to spend more cash flow on debt servicing and management plans to increase capital expenditures, there is less and less free cash flow for shareholders.

Risks

In my opinion, the main risk for business is associated with a slowdown in growth. The market expects a stronger increase in revenue and sales of electricity and reacts negatively when the actual result turns out to be worse than expected. Another risk is associated with the Calpine integration—the real synergy may not meet expectations, the process may be delayed, and new costs may arise. Electricity prices and their volatility strongly affect the company's results; the same applies to fuel prices, the growth of which leads to an increase in cost and operating costs and a reduction in the final margin. The remaining risks are related to the general macroeconomic situation, competition, possible operational incidents during the operation of nuclear power plants, and so on.

Final Thoughts

Recent financial results have turned out to be noticeably worse than expected, uncertainty around business growth rates has increased, net profit remains under pressure due to rising interest costs, management is making more conservative forecasts than before, and free cash flow for shareholders risks being severely reduced. The fair value of the shares has not changed since the last articles, and there is an upside to the current price, but due to the increased risks, I prefer to Hold.

data.title: The Era Of Tokenmaxxing
data.description:
A couple of weeks ago, Meta’s (META) internal dashboard for “tokenmaxxing” got a lot of attention after The Information reported (subscription required) on it. Although such tokenmaxxing phenomena only started getting attention recently, Shopify (SHOP) has been doing something similar since mid-2025. It is also hardly a Meta or Shopify-specific thing; Microsoft (MSFT) and Salesforce (CRM) are also apparently following something similar.

In a recent podcast, Shopify’s CTO, Mikhail Parakhin, shared some interesting data on AI tool usage at Shopify. Perhaps the most interesting data point is nearly everyone on Shopify is using at least one of the AI tools daily.

In the image below, the green line indicates the percentage of employees at Shopify who use at least one AI tool daily. In early 2026, only half of Shopify employees used one of the AI tools daily. Today, this number is basically approaching 100%. Opus 4.5 was basically a game changer for adoption, which has been driving the tokenmaxxing era at full speed.

Parakhin didn’t share exactly what each of the lines below denotes, but since he mentioned CLI-based tools are becoming more popular within Shopify, it’s probably fair to assume that the brown/tan line refers to Claude Code (Anthropic (ANTHRO)), and orange to Codex (OPENAI), which are currently used by ~70% and ~55% of employees, respectively, on a daily basis in Shopify.

Interestingly, he also indicated that tools that require IDEs, such as GitHub Copilot and Cursor are still growing, but they’re losing share rapidly (red line in the image). Any Shopify employee can use any AI tool with an unlimited token budget.

One interesting tidbit from the conversation is that even though everyone is using AI tools on a daily basis, there is a fair amount of inequality in usage. The top 10 percentile is actually growing significantly faster than the rest at Shopify, and I suspect the trend is similar in other organizations. From the podcast:

the super interesting part here is that you could see that the distribution becoming more and more skewed.The top percentiles grow faster. So that means- the people in the top ten percentile, their consumption grows faster than seventy-five and so forth. So, the distribution skews more and more towards the highest users, which is... I don’t know what it tells me. It’s like it feels not ideal, to be honest.

Looking at this data, I wondered if many tech companies will be forced to lay off the bottom 10 or 20 percentile of token spenders to create a budget for tokens for the top 10 or 20 percentile in the organization. Of course, the obvious retort to such a tokenmaxxing trend is that it may fall into the trap of Goodhart’s law: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure".

I’m sure these tech companies know that and will look closely into the data to see whether higher token usage correlates highly to productive work and not some mindless token spending for the sake of it. And if the data suggests that top percentile users do highly productive work with higher token spending, more budget will naturally follow to them until it stops making sense.

There are at least two other reasons why tokenmaxxing may actually make more sense than it may appear at first glance. The reality is tokens are likely still quite cheap; just yesterday, The Information reported that Cursor had a negative 23% gross margin in 2025.

While gross margins for Claude and Codex are likely a lot better than that, I think token buyers should be able to pick a lot of low-hanging fruit with such tools in the early stage of adoption. You can always optimize the costs later once you let everyone use these tools to their heart’s content for a few months and then look at the data closely to figure out the right optimization process later.

There is another interesting explanation why companies such as Meta, whose models are not as good at coding as Claude Code or Codex, are essentially using such wanton usage of tokens as training data for their own model. The Pragmatic Engineer Newsletter mentioned this potential rationale (emphasis mine):

“Putting a leaderboard in place was always going to incentivize much more AI usage. And more AI usage means producing a lot more real-world traces. These traces can then be used to train Meta’s next-generation coding model better.I believe this was the goal, even if no one said it out loud.It’s an expensive way to generate data for training, but if any company has the means to do so, it’s Meta.”

Indeed, having high-quality training data perhaps also explains SpaceX’s deal with Cursor. Kevin Kwok articulated this rationale really well in this post (emphasis mine):

Cursor must train its own models. And they’ve done this, starting with post-training an open source model in Composer 1, then extended pre-training and post-training for Composer 2, and now beginning to pre-train their own models from scratch. But it is one thing to build budget models with better margins and another to compete head to head on the state of the art. The compute expense is in the billions–if you can even get the compute.If Cursor believes it can compete at the highest levels but will see its position degrade without matching the AI labs on compute and model training, then Elon and SpaceX are their perfect complement.Elon merged xAI into SpaceX. Since then, its research leadership has been entirely hollowed out. A morbid joke is that it’s been like Iranian leadership: every day a new head of research is battlefield promoted, and every next day they are gone.In recent months Elon has become convinced of the importance of coding models, moving from a small team working on them to it being the entire lab’s priority. But building a coding model from scratch without any of the data or harness is hard to bootstrap. Even more so without research or product leadership.xAI has tremendous compute capacity, with plans to scale it as much or more as any of the other labs. Everything datacenter-related says xAI should get stronger every year, but it is clearly underperforming that compute capacity. It has the cheapest cost of compute perhaps not just because it is so good at building datacenters but also because no one is using them. It has been a lab with neither product/research directions nor heads. And it is not converging on its competitors–it is falling behind.Cursor is running out of time. xAI is in a race against time. Together they solve each other’s problems.Cursor gives SpaceX the research and product leadership that has shown it knows how to build in this space. It’s not a sure thing, but no team outside of the labs or China has done more. And the product immediately solves xAI’s coldstart problems around data and harness. Meanwhile, SpaceX gives Cursor the compute to compete long-term on both model training and inference scaling.Between them they have the complete loop. Neither does alone.

I have noticed many investors seem to be too eager to think the coding tool market is likely to be settled in a bit of a duopoly market between Claude Code and Codex. If that indeed turns out to be the case, future tokens may become quite expensive over time, as such a market structure may allow attractive margins for Claude Code and Codex.

However, for a market that has only started a couple of years ago, I suspect it is simply too early to declare winner(s) here. Anthropic was coding pilled more than any other lab, and as other labs are waking up to the extent of PMF coding tools (and the implications for such in the product downstream), I expect to see a concerted effort from other labs to make this space more competitive.

So, instead of the coding market being settled to a monopoly or duopoly, we may actually be in the early stage of a more competitive market. As Gavin Baker pointed out, in the future you may be either a token producer or a token consumer, and if you’re not a token producer, your ability to efficiently and effectively consume tokens as an organization may dictate your future. More competition, of course, would also be welcome if you are consuming tokens.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UMH Preferred: A 7.3% Yield And A Discount To Par
data.description:
Continuing to look after income opportunities, which also have an optionality of capital appreciation, I recently came across the preferred issue of UMH Properties (UMH). The said preferreds at present are trading at ~15% gap from the par and yielding around ~7.3% at market. The underlying dividend payout history is decent, with companies FFO being strong enough to sustain both the preferred dividend payouts and underlying dividends on common stocks. The entity operates as a REIT trust and therefore should necessarily distribute ~90% of its earnings (tax basis) to carry on its pass-through structures. This makes the preferred dividend treated largely as an ordinary income and thus neglects the benefit of qualified dividends, which happens to be the case with many other preferred stocks. That said, however, investors who look for diversification in their fixed income portfolio might consider taking a closer look at UMH preferred. A ~7.3% yield is still decent over treasury rates, and considering the historical track record along with a possibility of capital appreciation to the extent of the discount to par of these issues, should interest rate declines be taken in together makes the issue something that could be considered. However, it remains only as an addition to a fixed-income portfolio, among other issues, depending on the investor’s overall asset allocations.

Company Overview

UMH Properties is a REIT focused on purchasing, managing, and operating manufactured home communities of manufactured home buildings. The company typically leases out manufactured homesites to residents. The company presently operates roughly around ~145 manufactured housing communities, ~140 of which are wholly owned by the company.

With present interest rates standing at a relatively elevated range as compared to some time back, largely due to the last five years of rate hikes by the central government, the affordability of site-built houses for the customers has been reduced to an extent. It is important to note that manufactured housing is meaningfully cheaper than house-built housing, a point that I also highlighted while covering Legacy Housing Finance (LEGH), a manufactured home lender and builder. The same is the case for UMH as well; the difficulty in affording a costlier home, due to high finance rates, necessarily suggests an increased focus on cheaper alternatives. While it is true that the cost of funds rises for the companies involved in either of the two, a negative impact of rising interest rates in the case of manufactured housing largely gets offset by increased demand outlook from prospective customers due to better affordability.

While this is an average approximation, the gap between the prices of site-built houses and manufactured houses usually tends to be around 50%, with manufactured houses costing around ~50% less than site-built houses. This is excluding the cost of the land. These figures would differ depending on various contexts, but a broader point of a meaningful difference between the prices and, as a result, impacting the consumer preferences is understandable.

With reference to financials, UMH appears to be well capitalized with $900 million worth of equity (including preferential capital) on an asset base of ~$1.7 billion. The common equity capital is around ~$600 million, which, on an overall asset base, might appear to be somewhat less; however, when taking into account the nature of the company to be a REITs in substance, this does not seem to be an issue. The more important thing to consider is the interest & debt payments and underlying income and liquidity of the company in reference to them.

The total interest payment came in at around ~$30 million for FY25, which, when considering operating cash flows of around ~$80 million, was sufficiently covered. The common stock, in addition, has also declared and provided dividends constantly for the last many years, which could only have been provided with the company also being able to satisfy the requirements of both debt payments and preferred dividends. This dividend consistency provides for a strong signal of the financial strength of these REITs. Also, the marketable value of assets held by the company could possibly be higher than the current reported book values in the financial statements.

The debt maturities, in addition, also remain fairly moderate on a near- to medium-term basis, and therefore the company most likely would have enough liquidity for the time being to fund both preferred and common stock dividends. However, at the same time, the possibility of redemption of preference shares cannot be excluded either, in case of the availability of lower-cost financing options to the company. But $72 million in cash with limited debt repayment obligations adds to the comfort of an income-focused investor.

Preferred Stock of UMH Properties

UMH Properties 6.375% Series D Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (UMH.PR.D) presently trades at below its par value of $25, at around ~$21.8. The said issues pay a quarterly dividend of ~$0.3984 per share, making a dividend yield of ~7.3%. Kindly note that the underlying taxation structure of the company is that of a REIT trust, and therefore, the dividends might not get qualified treatment, which usually happens to be the case with other C Corporations. These dividends in general would largely be treated as ordinary income. That said, however, the present yield is still decent. Taking into account also the fact that these are at present trading at ~12.5% discount to their par value, implying a gap of ~15% from current price to par, and also the value at which these preferred used to trade some years back. That discount to par is largely the result of the increase in interest rates, resulting in an increase in the required rate of return (capitalization factor), causing a decline in market value to adjust with newer market interest rates.

These preferreds are perpetual in nature, meaning that they don’t have any maturity date. For approximation, the modified duration (interest rate sensitivity) of a perpetual instrument can be approximated as 1/yield. At the current yield of ~7.3%, this gives a modified duration of approximately ~13.7. The important caveat, however, is that these shares are redeemable/callable by the company at $25 par. This makes the effective duration lower than theoretical, particularly as the price approaches par, reflecting negative convexity. In short, the upside is therefore capped at par value, while the downside remains largely uncapped. Moreover, that option is active now; the company has the right to redeem these shares at any time at present or afterwards. It is understandable that the shares are already trading at a discount, reflecting a lower probability of redemption at this juncture, unless the interest rates fall further and the company finds it suitable to redeem the issue or refinance at a lower cost. This callability option of the company at present does not altogether disadvantage the investor, but a decline in rates from this level would change the course by increasing the possibility of company redeeming and, more importantly, reducing the allocation case of the investor by making the company's option to redeem more valuable.

Like I highlighted, the downside, however, is uncapped, and any increase in interest rates can lead to a bigger gap between the par value and market price of the preferred. These instruments, although hybrid in nature, behave more like a fixed-rate debt instrument, which is also ideal considering their profile as a fixed-return vehicle for the investors.

In my opinion, considering that at least at present there remains a meaningful gap with par/redemption value, and as a result, the investor stands to net the benefit of decline in interest rates till the issue reaches its par along with a ~7.3% yield, it remains an allocation that can be considered by an investor. In addition, the current preferred dividend payments of ~$20 million are well covered by the cash flows generated by the company. A REIT's FFO/Adjusted FFO better reflects the economic reality of these entities as compared to accounting earnings.

The above ~$80 million is the earnings attributable to common stockholders (post paying preferred dividends). Adding ~$20 million of preferred dividends provides us with an FFO of roughly $100 million attributable to equity capital of the company (both common stock and preferred shares). The $20 million of preferred dividend liability therefore implies a coverage of ~4.5 to 5x, which remains strong. This is further suggested by the uninterrupted preferred dividend payouts across the time. I have, in addition, already highlighted the consistency of common stock dividend payouts, which are only allowable when the dues to preferred dividends are covered. Kindly note that the current issue is cumulative preference shares.

Taking all of these aspects together, the track record of dividend payouts, the healthy preferred dividend coverage ratio, and a meaningful discount from redemption value, which for the time being allows an opportunity to benefit from the decline in market interest rates, can be considered by an income-seeking investor. The ~7.3% yield is decent, constituting a 300 basis point spread over the risk-free rates.

In addition, at present the possibility of declining rates is relatively high, with the probability of further rate hikes from the current range on a structural basis seemingly low. Even if the interest rates do not go down from present levels, the investor still nets 300 bps over treasury rates. If they go down, a possible capital appreciation would probably lead to meaningfully enhanced total returns. The risk-reward payoff therefore seems favorable at the present juncture. However, the convexity and duration are capped on the upside, and therefore, should the preferred shares appreciate in market price, an investor should actively look for other avenues for allocating the capital, where upside might still be present. At the same time, these therefore are not a complete proxy for betting on declining interest rates. These are a secondary factor in allocation in the present case. The primary case remains that of the attractiveness of the yield and the safety & consistency of the dividends.

Limitations to the Proposition

A 300-basis-point yield over risk-free rates necessarily carries an exposure to the underlying credit profile of the issuer. These preference shares stand below the fixed debt obligations of the company. Any deterioration in the credit profile of the company can severely impact the standing of these preference shares. The company is also primarily exposed to real estate cycles, and any sharp moderation or contraction in underlying manufactured housing community prices could lead to problems for the company and investors, both credit and equity. In addition to credit and company risks, the said preference shares do not offer much on the upside. The discount to par value is largely the cap on the upside for the issue. The company, in addition, has previously redeemed its other series preferred issues when they had the opportunity to refinance at lower costs. The option to redeem is active for the company, and should interest rates decline further, the company could possibly redeem the issue, leading investors to further look for capital deployment opportunities. The increase in market interest rates for any reason can further lead to a decline in market price and widening of the discount.

The said proposition also requires active monitoring and is not a buy-and-hold proposition. Should the gap between par and market price converge, an investor should look for better capital deployment opportunities.

In Conclusion

UMH, preferred at the present price of around or below ~$21.5–22, could be considered a fixed-income proposition for an income-oriented investor. The risks, however, should be kept in mind. In addition, any meaningful convergence of the current discount to par should be considered as a signal to book the income and profits and redeploy into other propositions. The thesis is not just on the basis of the ~7.3% yield on a standalone basis, but rather the current discount to par remains an equally important aspect. Also, this remains as an incremental addition to a fixed income allocation of an investor, a point also being suggestive of refraining from over-allocating to the issue.

data.title: LendingClub: Strong Originations Amid Private Credit Meltdown Ahead Of Rebrand
data.description:
Alongside fears about the impacts of AI on software and the broader macroeconomy, the other major worry bubbling up in the stock market is fear of another broad credit default. Private credit funds have faced outflows due to concerns about their exposure to software, while elsewhere in consumer credit, underwater car loans are threatening to cause defaults in an already weak economy.

Against this backdrop, consumer loan originator LendingClub (LC) has seen skeptical share price action this year. Yet after releasing strong Q1 results, the company shot up ~10%:

I last wrote a "Buy" article on LendingClub in January, when the stock was hovering around $19. This was before the AI-driven market meltdown and investors’ general withdrawal from small/mid-cap stocks and virtually anything that wasn’t a large-cap chipmaker. Still, with strong credit metrics and healthy originations, I believe LendingClub is worth a fresh look, especially after its healthy post-Q1 momentum. I’m reiterating my "Buy" opinion here.

At current share prices, there are plenty of tailwinds underneath LendingClub that support an upward re-rating of the stock. To me, the core bull case catalysts that investors should be aware of include:

  • Strong double-digit originations growth. The company’s lifeblood is in originating new loans, which forms the core of the company’s non-interest fee-based income and is largely divested to other investors. Amid a choppy economy, borrower demand remains strong, and LendingClub has retained >30% originations growth.
  • Potential increase in capital from private credit flight. Concerns over corporate loans in the private credit space may actually push yield seekers to other credit alternatives, such as what LendingClub offers.
  • Deposit growth and a rebrand to Happen Bank. LendingClub is evolving into a formidable neobank, and its high-yield savings account (currently offering ~4.0% APY) is attracting more deposits. This is reducing LendingClub’s cost of capital and raising net interest margin. The company is planning to rebrand to Happen Bank, reflecting the company's shift toward becoming a digital bank rather than simply a lending platform.
  • Strong credit quality. Delinquency rates remain in the low single digits and underperform consumer loans of similar credit score vintages, reinforcing investor trust in LendingClub’s origination process.

Despite its heft as a growth story, LendingClub actually trades like a value name. As shown below, the company has maintained its full-year FY26 outlook calling for 26% originations growth and $1.65 to $1.80 in pro forma EPS.

So at current post-earnings share prices near $19, LendingClub trades at an 11.3x FY26 P/E. For a company that is proving to grow originations at such a strong clip while maintaining healthy net interest margins in a declining Fed rate environment, this remains a compelling buy.

Q1 Download

Let’s now go through LendingClub’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

LendingClub's total revenue grew 16% y/y to $252.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $249.1 million (+14% y/y) by a two-point margin. Net interest income (which is driven by interest rate spreads plus total loans held for investment) grew 18% y/y to $176.2 million. Meanwhile, non-interest revenue (which consists of origination fees) grew 12% y/y to $76.0 million, which is 30% of the company's total.

Originations, which are the core source of LendingClub's growth, continued at a rapid pace. The company issued $2.67 billion in new loans this quarter, growing 31% y/y (versus 40% growth in Q4).

Importantly, appetite for LendingClub assets continues to grow. The company sold over half (52%) of new originations to investors, and as we previously mentioned, we expect that the company may enjoy strong institutional demand for its loans as capital shifts out of private commercial credit. The chart below, meanwhile, showcases that LendingClub's credit quality remains well above its peer set across credit score classes:

Importantly, even in a declining interest rate environment, LendingClub is maintaining rich net interest margins. The company's increased pool of deposits has allowed it to lower its interest-bearing deposit costs to 3.60%, a 31bps reduction in cost of capital from a y/y basis. Meanwhile, interest-earning assets generated a 9.31% return on the company's loans held for investment.

This has produced a healthy 6.28% net interest margin, rising 31bps y/y. And as a clear measure of how profitable LendingClub's balance sheet is becoming, we note that its return on average equity in the quarter rose to 14.5%, up 260bps sequentially and 130bps y/y:

We expect LendingClub's rebrand as Happen Bank and an influx of deposits to continue lowering LendingClub's cost of capital, as consistent ROE expansion lies ahead.

Risks and Key Takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind. Already, there are fledgling signs that a weak economy may be impacting consumer credit. As reported recently by the Wall Street Journal, a batch of pandemic-era auto loans are now underwater as borrowers struggle to make payments on cars that were purchased at a high price and have depreciated substantially. A rise in defaults may impact LendingClub's ability to divest its originations to third parties, and it may be forced to expand its balance sheet to keep originating at the same pace. And in a lower interest rate environment, we may also see banks become more competitive in offering personal lending and credit card products to their consumers, which may compete against LendingClub's current pace of originations.

That said, I see more upside than risk in this stock, especially at a very favorable ~11x forward P/E ratio. Stay long here ahead of LendingClub's rebrand and business model shift, and keep riding LendingClub's post-earnings momentum higher.

data.title: Pathward Financial: Credit Deterioration Is Concerning (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Pathward Financial (CASH) have been a moderate performer over the past year, up 11% but lagging the market’s more substantial recovery. Shares fell sharply after the company reported Q1 earnings. While results were broadly in line with expectations, credit trends continue to degrade, and this remains a key area of concern for me. I last covered shares in January, when I rated CASH a “Hold” given weakening credit quality and a low level of reserves, offset by a fair valuation. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Pathward. I am increasingly cautious.

In the company’s second quarter, Pathward Financial earned $3.35 per share, which matched expectations as revenue grew 5% to $276 million. While net income was down 3% from last year given increased credit costs, EPS was up 7% given the lower share count from its aggressive buyback program. However, warning signs are building in its loan book that are harder to ignore with each passing quarter.

Pathward has a stable deposit base of $5.85 billion, up 1% from last year. Interestingly, its cost of deposits actually increased by 14bps sequentially to 1.63%, primarily due to seasonal noise. Still, this was down just 12bps from last year, reflecting limited pull-through of rate cuts. CASH’s deposits have a relatively small beta to fed funds, and on net, Fed rate cuts are a negative for its business as a result.

On the asset side, it is carrying $4.9 billion of loans, up 9% from last year. The pace of loan growth was impressive, considering a fairly muted lending environment. However, one concern is that loan growth has been so robust in part because its underwriting standards are too lax, leading to greater potential losses (I will discuss more below). Originations of $1.3 billion were up from $900 million last year, so Pathward shows no sign of slowing down activity.

CASH’s adjusted net interest margin (“NIM”) was 5.32%, up 23bps from last year. On a published basis, however, NIM was down about 49bps from last year. It sold its consumer finance loan portfolio in October, which was higher yielding, and that compositional change is a headwind that is excluded from the adjusted metric.

Looking deeper at its balance sheet, the majority of its portfolio sits inside structured finance. Much of this segment is focused on funding leveraged acquisitions, rural borrowers, and alternative energy projects, some of which have come under pressure from shifting government support. Beyond this, I am concerned that the portfolio is getting riskier, just as credit quality is worsening. While its portfolio is up $700 million from a year ago, the portion of the portfolio guaranteed by the government has been cut in half. As such, an even greater share of the portfolio faces credit risk, which should increase losses, all else equal.

These mid-sized business borrowers have often turned to private credit for financing, and we have seen increased losses there. One concern I have is that the amount of capital that entered the sector led to a competition to lend and a “race to the bottom” in standards, now leading to more losses. Given this, I worry about the pace of Pathward’s loan growth. CASH also has some implicit private credit exposure in its warehouse business. Here, it finances portfolios of loans. Now, these structures have significant subordination, so I do not see significant losses. CASH has never faced a loss here, in fact. It would likely take a severe downturn to drive losses, which is not my base case. These loans are about 12% of its business.

Beyond this, Pathward also has an equipment and a working capital business, and both lending types are generally low risk. However, again, this is an area of increased competition from private credit. While losses should be manageable, I expect a worse performance than has been the historic norm. Indeed, it is difficult to ignore the weakening in credit quality we are already seeing.

The company saw both an increase in early-stage delinquencies (30-59 days) as well as 90+ day loans that have been flipped to “nonperforming loan” (“NPL”) status. While some of the new delinquencies resolved in Q2, the growth in 90+ day delinquencies is a concerning sign. NPLs are now 2.39% of loans, which is up from just 88bps a year ago. NPLs have nearly tripled over the past year, which is a deeply concerning pace of degradation. The sequential weakening in Q1 primarily related to its commercial finance portfolio. Net charge-offs also rose to $16 million from $13 million last year.

Management described the credit environment as “stable,” which is arguably overly sanguine given the trend we have seen in NPLs. Despite this commentary, CASH increased its credit provision, speaking to a bit more caution. Against this rising pool of net charge-offs, the company has just $98 million of allowances, providing less than 1x coverage of NPLs. This is exceptionally weak, and I prefer to see 2x coverage. Allowances are 2.02% of loans, down 28bps from last year. Allowing reserves to decline over the past year despite the dramatic increase in NPLs is a highly aggressive decision in my view. I would like to see CASH at least carry 2-2.5x years of charge-offs, implying at least $30-$40 million more reserves are needed.

Separately, tax services revenue has been a nice source of growth, up 13%, and tax products added $11 million to fee income relative to last year. Pathward is also benefiting from the fact that tax refunds are running a bit larger this year, given the impacts of the 2025 tax law. As a result, it has been able to grow its refund advance loans with originations of about $200 million from last year. So far this year, noninterest income is 46% of revenue. This ratio will drop into the mid-30% area by year-end as tax fees are disproportionately received in Q2.

While its reserves are weak, the company maintains a solid capital position with a common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 12.65%. Capital remains well above regulatory minimums. Given its loan growth and composition of loan balances, I would like to see CET1 remain solidly above 11%, so CASH has plenty of headroom, which enables it to return capital to shareholders. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 9% over the past year. It also pays a token dividend of 0.2%.

Alongside results, the company reiterated guidance for $8.55-$9.05 in EPS. Back in January, I was targeting $8.35-$8.85 in 2026 EPS, but this assumed rate cuts that now appear unlikely to appear. That said, credit trends and its reserve posture are increasingly problematic in my view. I see $8.45-$8.85 in EPS this year, leaving shares at about 10x earnings. However, if there is a downturn or a weakening in private credit fundamentals, I see CASH as being one of the more challenged names I follow, with significant reserve builds likely to be needed. This downside risk has only grown over the past quarter.

Given this, I view 10x as a full multiple, and with its reserves, I view risk/reward as uncompelling. While it may be too cautious, I am moving shares to "Sell." With many financials in the 9-12x area, I would rather invest elsewhere in the sector and take less credit risk at a similar price. Given the nature of its balance sheet, I do not see a case for multiple expansion, and as such, I am moving shares to a “Sell.” I would avoid CASH and want to see shares closer to 8x, or ~$70, before considering buying.

data.title: Deep Sail Capital Q1 2026 Investor Letter
data.description:
Investors,

In the first quarter of 2026, Deep Sail Capital Partners (the "Fund") returned -17.8% net of fees while averaging 84% net long exposure. Since the end of the first quarter the fund has returned 20.4% net of fees and now sits flat year to date. Please consult your individual capital account statements for your individual net returns.

Russell 2000 0.9% 25.7% 14.8% 8.9% -18.7% -14.7% -1.9% 0.7%
Russell Mid Cap Growth Index -1.4% 15.4% 19.4% 11.4% -16.3% -4.4% -6.5% -1.9%

In the first quarter, the fund underperformed both of its benchmarks, the Russell 2000 Mid Cap Growth Index and the Russell 2000 Index. The result was somewhat atypical, as performance tracked broadly in line with benchmarks for most of the quarter before several positions experienced material drawdowns late in March. This follows a long stretch of seven consecutive quarters of positive net returns for the fund.

Performance in the quarter was driven by weakness in the long portfolio, partially offset by gains in the short portfolio. The drawdown in long positions was concentrated toward quarter-end and coincided with broader market volatility surrounding the Iran war selloff. Notably, these losses have completely reversed in the second quarter as of the market close on April 23rd.

While the short portfolio generated positive returns, as would be expected in a declining market, the magnitude of the contribution was below my expectations. Following strong performance in the prior quarter and given the significant declines of many lower-quality, long-duration equities (a source of the fund's short exposure) in Q4 2025, the short book entered the quarter with significant gains from Q4. As a result, the short return did not materially offset the losses generated on the long side as has historically been the case.

Market Commentary – AI is overwhelming everything

My quarterly commentaries have been very skewed toward the negatives over the last 12 months, but this time I will shift a bit to cover why AI and the associated data center ramps might just be the only thing that matters to the market trajectory for the next few years.

The global economy has entered a period of structural transformation that transcends the typical ebbs and flows of the business cycle with the adoption of LLMs, AI agents, and soon physical AI. The unprecedented capex cycle required to build the physical infrastructure of the machine-intelligence age is beyond massive. For investors, the data center ramp is no longer merely a "story" but rather a fundamental economic driver upon which all future economic activity is being built.

The scale of the current investment in AI infrastructure is perhaps the most visible indicator of its economic dominance. The Mag7 have shifted from being capital-light software providers to some of the most capital-intensive private enterprises in history. By 2026, the combined annual capex of the five largest U.S. hyperscalers—Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Oracle—is projected to reach between $660 billion and $690 billion. This represents a near-doubling of spending from 2025 levels and is more than four times the annual capital investment of the entire publicly traded U.S. energy sector.

AI Capex Investment Forecasts

Source 2025 Est. 2026 Forecast Long-Term Projection (through 2030)
Dell'Oro Group $489.5 Billion ~$1 Trillion (Global) $1.7 Trillion (Annual) by 2030
Bloomberg Intelligence $450 Billion $750 Billion (Top 14 firms) $3 Trillion (Aggregate) by 2029
Goldman Sachs / Analysts $410 Billion $700 Billion (Hyperscalers) $5–$7 Trillion (Cumulative) by 2030
JPMorgan $342 Billion (Hyperscalers) ~$500 Billion (Reinvestment) $5 Trillion (Cumulative) by 2030

This surge is not a temporary spike but a reflection of a fundamental shift in the lifecycle of computing assets, or as Jensen likes to say, " accelerated computing." This level of spending is fundamentally rewriting the risk-reward profile of the equity market. While these companies are technically categorized as "Technology," their balance sheets and operational hurdles now mirror those of heavy industry like the railroads and oil refineries before them. The sheer magnitude of the capex can't be funded from the Mag 7 cash flows alone. Significant debt will be required as the need for AI compute continues to expand. That means that the stability of the credit markets is now inextricably linked to the continued demand for AI compute; if the demand or use cases falter, we might hit a brick wall economically.

On top of the availability of the capital to do all this is the question of "Are there enough materials and energy?" This lack of components and availability of energy (both from an energy production and energy grid capacity standpoint) has become the limiting factor in the AI buildout.

While the capex story is clear for at least the next few years, there are still massive questions that are not answered around post-AI employment, productivity gains, the reshoring of high-value production (like silicon, rare earths, drones, and nuclear materials), and how/when physical AI will have its “ChatGPT” moment.

As we look across the next few years, the reality is that we are witnessing the birth of a new kind of civilization, one built on a substrate of silicon with massive intelligence limited by compute. Will the AI Supercycle eventually crash and cause a recession? At this point I would say it is hard to tell exactly if or when that will happen, as we are still barely getting into the real AI Supercycle.

The impact of AI is just so large relative to any other market forces at this time, that it may truly be all that matters to drive the market up or down.

Long Portfolio Summary

Vertiv Holdings (VRT) 2.6% SANUWAVE Health -4.2%
Lumentum Holdings (LITE) 1.0% ClearPoint Neuro (CLPT) -2.6%

In the first quarter, the long portfolio underperformed both of our benchmarks, the Russell 2000 Index and the Russell Midcap Growth Index, by a significant margin. There were a few large detractors at the end of the quarter, including On Holding (ONON), Credo Technology (CRDO), Amplitech Group, and Sanuwave Holdings. Amplitech and Sanuwave were both down on negative reactions to their Q4 earnings reports, which I believe were both unwarranted. Credo Technologies was down over 50% from its ATH by the end of March on the narrative that “Cooper is dead” in data centers. That narrative has since changed in Q2 with the market beginning to realize that Credo’s ZeroFlap optics platform is a real second leg for the company. Credo went on to make a transformative acquisition of Dust Photonics just last week, which not only makes Credo a viable optics supplier but also creates a vertically integrated platform for connectivity in the data center space.

In Q1, the fund closed one position and opened five new positions. The new positions in Q4 included AmpliTech, MDA Space, and Micron (MU).

After several years of watching Constellation Software and following the company, in Q1 we finally had the opportunity to buy shares at a reasonable valuation. We have long owned Topicus Software (which is a spinout from Constellation Software), but only recently did the valuation and forward IRR of Constellation Software land it in a viable range for the fund to purchase shares. I believe the AI narrative around VMS software is largely wrong, and AI will provide a nice tailwind for the industry over the next five years.

Current Position: AmpliTech – Emerging Growth

AmpliTech Group (AMPG) operates a high-quality, diversified business model centered on designing, developing, and manufacturing advanced RF and microwave signal-processing components and systems. The company serves multiple end markets, which include satellite communications, 5G/6G telecom, quantum computing, defense, aerospace, and space. It operates through five divisions: AmpliTech Inc., Specialty Microwave, Spectrum Semiconductor Materials, AmpliTech Group Microwave Design Center, and the fast-growing AmpliTech Group 5G Division.

AmpliTech's 5G ORAN product is the material driver of the current revenue growth that the company has seen. ORAN (or O-RAN) stands for "Open Radio Access Network." ORAN, led by the O-RAN Alliance, is an agreed-upon framework that has been adopted by telecom operators for 5G networks, mainly in non-US markets. 5G ORAN is an open interface for building a 5G network. This allows telecom operators to mix and match vendors, reducing their dependence on proprietary, single-vendor systems while improving the supply chain and driving down costs for the telecom operators.

A key point about true 5G networks vs. 4G or 4G LTE is that due to the coverage efficiency, bandwidth used, and latency requirements, 5G networks will have materially more endpoints (RAN) devices required than 4G networks. This means that ORAN makers will be in high demand as the replacement cycle from 4G to 5G continues, which is a massive tailwind for AmpliTech for the next 3 years.

The company is currently forecasting at least $50m in 2026 revenue (100% growth over 2025), which will be driven by two large LOI's that they have secured. The company announced in 2025 they have received an LOI for $78 million in ORAN radio systems with an Asian telco and a $40m LOI for a North American MNO. These LOIs are both just getting ramped up and will support the 2026 revenue growth.

High quality business model

AmpliTech's business segmentation structure creates natural cross-selling opportunities and a somewhat resilient revenue base, although their 5G ORAN business is lumpy. The legacy components business (low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) and low-noise blocks (LNBs)) provides stable, high-margin recurring demand from satellite, defense, and test/measurement customers. Their 5G systems business, which was materially scaled up in 2025, delivers a scalable, high-volume platform revenue opportunity within the 5G Open RAN (ORAN) space.

Fiscal 2025 revenue reached a record $25.2 million, up approximately 165% year-over-year from $9.5 million in 2024. Growth was driven by strong demand for LNA/LNB products, expanded 5G product lines, and a rebound in the Spectrum division's Asian markets. Gross profit rose to $6.0 million (from $3.5 million), but gross margin compressed to 23.9% (from 36.7%).

This compression in gross margin was largely a one-off event tied to three factors: (1) elevated legal and professional fees associated with capital-raising activities and compliance during the year's rapid scaling; (2) one-time costs from the registered direct and rights offerings that strengthened the balance sheet; and (3) the deliberate early-stage ramp-up of carrier-grade 5G ORAN radio production, including competitive pricing to secure Tier-1 operator relationships and initial manufacturing scale-up inefficiencies. Management has explicitly guided that these headwinds are transitory and gross margins should return to the ~40% range in the back half of 2026 and be trending toward the 30–50% telecom industry benchmark as fixed costs are absorbed and pricing normalizes on established contracts. This margin improvement should be steadily driven as the company shifts from market-penetration pricing and low-volume pilot deployments to recurring, high-volume orders with better gross margins.

The model is further strengthened by vertical integration with design and manufacturing in the U.S., patented front-end and LNA technologies, and a transition from pure component supplier to full systems provider. AmpliTech's products are 100% American-made, which may be a key selling point with US telcom providers due to onshoring efforts in the future.

Outstanding management

Amplitech is led by its founder, chairman, president, and CEO, Fawad Maqbool. Fawad has 30 years of microwave engineering experience with dual BSEE degrees in microwave and biomedical engineering. He has scaled the business from nothing to $50m in revenue forecasted for 2026. Maqbool founded the company in 2002 and has steered it from a bootstrapped startup to a NASDAQ-listed growth platform while maintaining hands-on oversight of RF innovation.

Outside of Fawad, AmpliTech's leadership team combines deep technical expertise with proven operational discipline. Complementing him is COO Jorge Flores, who joined in 2021 with over 30 years of operations and program management experience across complex manufacturing environments.

Management's execution has been strong within the last few years. In 2025 they delivered 165% revenue growth while simultaneously certifying the industry's first U.S.-manufactured 64T64R CAT-B massive MIMO ORAN radio. This 5g ORAN product allowed them to secure the $78m LOI with a Tier-1 operator. While their 4Q results showed a gross margin decline due to the 5G ramp, they continue to be transparent and unwavering about the long-term vision for the company. As they approach a positive operating profit (likely later in 2026), they have proactively raised capital on favorable terms to fund the 5G ramp once the business inflects.

Guidance has been consistently met or raised, and the team's willingness to accept short-term gross-margin dilution for strategic design wins demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of carrier procurement cycles. AmpliTech's management is well positioned to convert early ORAN leadership into multi-year, high-margin revenue.

My biggest issue with management is their IR has not been great. They had not scheduled an earnings call for Q4 results and released the results, and the stock sold off 30% after the results. Then, in what seemed kind of reactionary, they scheduled a call to discuss the results. They also do not do a very good job at getting back to investors who have questions. This is not an idea killer to me if the business decisions seem correct and they are focusing on execution, but I do think they could improve IR.

Substantial long term growth prospects

AmpliTech sits at the epicenter of the secular shift to Open RAN in 5G infrastructure. Traditional 4G RAN was monolithic and proprietary, locking operators into single-vendor ecosystems with high costs and limited flexibility. ORAN disaggregates the radio, baseband, and software layers, enabling multi-vendor interoperability, which is why Amplitech has succeeded in selling into much larger MNO telecom companies. ORAN is an interesting product within 5G that is more flexible and adoptable than its 4G counterpart.

Specifically, AmpliTech's 5G ORAN product offers ~30% cost savings, faster innovation, and domestic supply chain security, which is a critical sales advantage over Chinese competition due to geopolitical concerns.

As I mentioned above, the key investment insight about the 5G opportunity ahead of AmpliTech is understanding the 5G infrastructure rollout versus the previous 4G infrastructure. A key differentiator is the dramatically higher density of endpoints required for 5G versus 4G. 5G demands gigabit-per-second speeds, ultra-low latency, and higher bandwidths. These requirements force denser network topologies as higher-frequency bands, which have shorter propagation distances. The shorter distances of propagation force telecom companies to deploy many more small cells and massive MIMO radios per square kilometer (5-20x depending on the buildings and structures nearby). This density is required for applications that require low latency and fast two-way signal strength, like autonomous vehicles, AR/VR, drones, and other new technologies.

Industry forecasts project the global 5G infrastructure market growing at a 22%+ CAGR by 2035. ORAN will be a major subset of that growth. AmpliTech's U.S.-made, OTIC-tested, O-RAN-compliant, and interoperability-proven device positions the company as a rare domestic pure-play beneficiary.

Current analyst consensus (one covering firm) forecasts align with management's reaffirmed FY2026 revenue guidance of at least $50 million (roughly 2x FY2025), which to me is an easy forecast to hit with just the existing two LOIs. But the company recently discussed that they are in the process of RFPs and other discussions for additional LOIs on their 5G ORAN products. I expect we'll hear more about further LOIs by mid-summer, which could materially bolster the 2025 revenue and support another sizable revenue growth in 2026.

The Legacy RF and Spectrum divisions should continue growing mid-teens, providing a stable base while 5G systems become the dominant, higher-margin contributor. Gross margins normalize to ~40%+ by H2 2026 and sustain or expand thereafter, driving robust free-cash-flow generation and potential for margin expansion through operating leverage.

Reasonable valuation

AmpliTech reported what was viewed by investors as a bad quarter in Q4 2025. Although revenue grows 111% y-o-y, it sequentially declined for the last two quarters. On top of that, the gross margins went down to 24%. Management did a fairly good job at explaining the Q4 numbers, although the conference call was a week after the earnings announcement.

The 2026 revenue guidance of “at least $50m” in revenue is still intact, and management confirmed that on the call. There will likely be some lumpy quarters as they ship orders against both of their LOIs. Investors never like a lumpy business, so I suspect that is why its current enterprise value is just $46m.

Below is my base case forecast for the company based on the existing 5g ORAN LOIs and the other business lines.
