Note that the company completed a rights offering in January 2026, in which they sold 2.2m shares at $4 which came with two rights: Series A right with $5 strike and expiry in July 2026, Series B right with $6 strike and expiry in November 2026. This would be about a 18% share dilution if the rights were executed, which suggests some resistance around $5-6/share. What is interesting about this rights offering is, why did management execute this with such short expiry times? $5 in July 2026 and then $6 in November 2026. It is a bit of foreshadowing from management, about what they see on the horizon for 2026.

There is a bull case where they receive new LOIs for 5G ORAN, as management has suggested on the recent call. If that is the case, their revenue growth might be significantly higher than 30% in 2027 and 2028.

The only analyst covering AmpliTech (Maxim Group) revised down their price target from $9 to $7 a share after the Q4 2025 earnings report on margin concerns. Currently Maxim Group is forecasting 2026 EBITDA at $11m, which would put the valuation at 3.5x 2026 EBITDA. I am a bit suspicious of that figure, as I think it assumes full-year gross profit at 40%, instead of a blended full-year number closer to 35%. Even so, my 2026 EV/EBITDA multiple is just 10.3x, and then it drops to 4.4 for 2027. This is more than reasonable if the company can continue its path to EBITDA and cash flow breakeven while growing the topline at +30%. I believe shares are worth north of $6 (210% upside) once the company can prove out gross margins and scale their 5G ORAN business.

Catalysts and Conclusion

  • 5G ORAN LOIs: With over $100m in LOIs in hand, the main issue will be timing around projects and rollouts. Management expects to get through a good chunk of the LOIs in 2025, so I assume we will see a few quarters above $10m revenue.
  • New LOIs for 5G ORAN: The 5G rollout globally is still in the expansion stages globally, where cost might become a key factor in new projects, which is exactly where AmpliTech is strongest.

The worst seems to be behind AmpliTech in terms of the valuation drop after the recent earnings. Q1 has historically not been a great quarter for the business, but they expect to begin to start executing against the LOIs they have in hand, so I would expect to see a solid Q1 report, which will put investors at ease with the story.

The short portfolio had a stellar return in Q4 2025, which generated a 7.4% contribution in the face of the Russell 2000 being up 2.2%. Coming off the back of that, the mini-bubble in low-quality, long-duration stocks had largely already subsided. The core drivers to the short book in 2025 were quantum-related stocks, which by early 2026 I had either closed or they saw the downside volatility decline significantly. While the short portfolio contributed positively to the overall returns of the fund in Q1, there was less opportunity than we saw in much of 2025 for “easy” short setups both fundamentally and technically.

data.title: Textron: Quietly Lining Up A Q1 Beat
data.description:
Textron (TXT) is a diversified multi-line industrial company that has a large product offering across aircraft, defense, and other industrial verticals. As a large supplier to the US government, the company has clear visibility into growth for one of its largest divisions, Bell, to begin to ramp on production for a multi-year period on its next-gen helicopter program. With the Textron Aviation business growing at a comfortable low-double-digit rate and a Q1 earnings beat on the way, the stock looks well-set up to reverse its underperformance.

Business Profile

Textron is a diversified industrial company best known for aerospace and defense systems, as well as specialty vehicles. It produces everything from Private jets under the Cessna and Beechcraft brands, military drones and related equipment, and helicopters under the Bell brand, among other vehicles and aircraft.

Textron has four segments - Textron Aviation, Bell, Textron Systems, and Industrial. There are two very small segments that don't move the needle on results - eAviation and Finance. The company will be reclassifying eAviation into Textron Aviation going forward for future quarters.

In terms of revenue mix, Textron Aviation is approximately 40.3% of total revenue (soon to be 40.5% post the reclassification of eAviation), 28.9% Bell, 21.7% Industrial, 8.4% Textron Systems, and the balance is Finance. It should also be noted that given it produces things like military drones, equipment and helicopters, the company has a 27% exposure to the US government, which is its single largest customer. The rest of the customer exposure is commercial. It's important to note that with a business like this, Textron also has access to supplying the aftermarket, which is higher margin and creates years of revenue visibility. Aftermarket makes up nearly 34% of Textron Aviation revenue and is growing at a stable 6%. While it may not have the revenue torque that the core aircraft business has, it is much less cyclical.

In terms of geographical mix, the United States is 69.5% of total company revenue. Europe is approximately 8.7%, Latin & South America is 8.6% and other international is 13.2%. This means the company, especially given the US government exposure, is heavily reliant on domestic defense budget growth in order to see efficacy in certain operations like Bell, which was awarded the MV-75 contract several years ago.

The backlog is also important to monitor for Textron. The total backlog for the company has been growing and remains near the high watermark. Textron Aviation has been rather stable, just not growing. Bell has been increasing, especially as the prototyping of the MV-75 nears completion. Textron systems is what has seen some backlog growth, but this company is largely stable.

Source: Author Compiled

Another view of the backlog can be seen below:

Source: Author Compiled

With a company like Textron, there is going to be a considerable amount of sensitivity to macroeconomic conditions, both geopolitical and industrial manufacturing activity. In a time of economic contraction, demand for Textron Aviation aircraft will decline and is what creates cyclical risk for this business. For example, when PMIs were low in 2024, Textron Aircraft was down 6% that year.

Financial Profile & Q1 Earnings Setup

The company provides overarching financial guidance, but also provides significant segment-level detail, which is helpful. Recall the guide:

  • FY26
    • Revenue $15.5 billion (+5% y/y)
    • Adj. EPS of $6.40-$6.60
    • $700-$800 million in manufacturing cash flow before pension contributions
    • $50 million pension contribution

Source: Investor Presentation

Thus, in building forward estimates for the year ahead, we have a lot to work with.

Textron each quarter puts out their deliveries, so it's important for us to unpack these deliveries so we might better understand deliveries and trends. Total company deliveries reached a high point last quarter, driven largely by more Bell commercial deliveries. Military units did decrease notably, but the number of units in that mix are rather low. This relates to the ongoing MV-75 Aircraft program for Bell whereby they are delivering next-generation helicopters.

Source: Author Compiled

To double click on that, the program should accelerate and in particular this sets up a catalyst for shares. The company should receive the Low-Rate Initial Production (LRIP) contract later this year or in early 2027. The company remains in the prototype phase of this program, but as soon as production can begin, the company can start to close the gap between minimal operating revenue and full-scale production by 2030/2031. Most of the backlog at Bell is for the MV-75 program at this stage. Also, it's worth noting that in 2025, Bell revenue was +28% and commercial helicopters were +9%, so the segment is not starved for growth, despite the slow progress on the MV-75.

Textron aviation deliveries were steady and that's the right way to think about this going forward. Another year of mid-single-digit% on aftermarket, with scope for upside to high-single digit revenue growth, with a starting point of mid-teens growth on aircraft is how I'm thinking about it. I've wrapped Textron eAviation into Textron aviation but it is immaterial. I'm modeling $1.37 billion for Textron Aviation in Q1.

For Bell, I'm modeling the segment guidance to beat at 11% growth. This results in Bell revenue of $1.09 billion. For Textron Systems, this should continue to quietly compound at 12.5%, midpoint of their segment guidance, while Industrial should at least keep pace with guidance at 5% growth. There is potential for this segment to miss the mark given the relative instability of geopolitics in Q1, but PMIs did hold up and that's not something to look past. As we put all of this together, we arrive at total revenue, including finance, of $3.63 billion versus street at $3.51 billion.

I find street's revenue estimate to be very interesting. Street seems to be under-modeling Textron Aviation. There seems to be a very wide disconnect here which sets up an easy top line beat.

From a segment margin standpoint, given the good growth in aircraft and a higher relative aftermarket mix, I'm modeling Textron Aviation margins at 11.9%. I am modeling Bell margins to be down y/y from 9.2% last year to 8.7% this year, but higher than guidance on the full year at 8.5%. Textron Systems should continue to see margin accretion and I am modeling 14% segment margin, while Industrial should also see higher margin levels with the PMI inflection y/y starting to show through profitability. I'm modeling +100bps in industrial to 5.2%, ahead of guidance at 4.5%. The eAviation business takes a loss, which per quarter is worth about $16 million, which is effectively offset by the quarterly profit of the finance business.

Total segment profit amounts to $379 million. After factoring in below the line items which they've cleanly guided to, we shake out to $239 million in adjusted net income with adj. EPS of $1.42 versus street at $1.30. This beat magnitude is similar to other beats in the past, seen below.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Valuation

In turning to the company's balance sheet, Textron has $1.85 billion in net debt, comprised of $3.9 billion in total debt less $2 billion in cash. With annual adjusted EBITDA at nearly $1.7 billion, the company is essentially not levered and has quite the clean balance sheet to support future growth activity.

The issue with this company's valuation is that because it is a diversified industrial company, the range of products they produce are not necessarily synergistic. They produce everything from golf carts to military helicopters and this is an overhang on the multiple. It's not pureplay A&D so it did not have the same torque to the upside during the Iran war as the likes of Lockheed (LMT) or Northrop Grumman (NOC) despite having very similar profit streams.

On a comparable basis, valuation is as follows:

  • Lockheed (LMT) - trades at 17.2x forward earnings
  • Northrop (NOC) - trades at 20.58x forward earnings
  • RTX Corp (RTX) - trades at 30.5x forward earnings
  • General Dynamics (GD) - trades at 19.2x forward earnings

This is relative to TXT today trading at 15.7x forward earnings. For a top line growth trajectory of 5% with double-digit earnings growth after guiding for just 6.5% growth, the Q1 beat can go a long way towards revising estimates higher on the street and garnering multiple expansion.

I like the stock at $6.75 in EPS this year, driven by Textron Aviation growth and higher margins, at a 17.5x multiple. This creates a price target of $118 versus its current level of $88 for 34% upside. This would also take out the past two highs it's had around $100/share. A bounce off of the 200-day moving average here with a good earnings report can certainly help to catalyze that. I don't expect a guidance raise post Q1, despite a 12c beat, but I do expect it after Q2 should the operating environment fundamentals persist.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

The primary risk associated with Textron is the US government. At 27% of revenue, there is significant customer concentration risk. While it is not uncommon for A&D-linked stocks to have significant exposures to the US government, it is a risk no less. Should defense spending slow or not grow, the company could be adversely impacted.

Next, the company is exposed to cyclicality of business jets through Cessna and Beechcraft in Textron Aviation. Order books can only tell the story for so long before conversion on the backlog actually has to happen. Failure to convert some or several craft in the backlog could be a downside risk for the company.

Finally, the company is a diversified multi-line industrial. It's subject to a large host of macroeconomic factors including industrial PMIs, in market inventory of aircraft, tariffs and even simple GDP growth. Adverse macroeconomic conditions can have outsized negative impacts on the company, particularly within Textron Aviation.

Conclusion

Textron looks poised to beat Q1, largely driven by the strength of the aircraft business within the Aviation segment. Coupled with a longer-dated increasing importance of the Bell segment, with the MV-75 looking to finally start getting into the production phase, there are real earnings drivers on the horizon that can propel this stock higher. After having lagged versus pure A&D peers, Textron can recoup some of the underperformance later this week when it reports.
